description:
A security engineer needs to rotate production API credentials that are stored on-disk in JSON
    files.  The rotation must be fully auditable, schema-validated, and 100 % reproducible from a
    Linux shell.

    1.  Directory layout that already exists
        • /home/user/cred_rotation/old_creds.json         ← current (version 1) credential file  
        • /home/user/cred_rotation/creds_schema.json      ← JSON-Schema used for validation  

    2.  What you have to do
        a. Validate old_creds.json against creds_schema.json, using only tools that are available
           in a standard Ubuntu image (e.g. jq; **do not** install global npm modules or similar).
           If the document is valid, record a log line (see logging format below).

        b. Create a fresh credential document called
              /home/user/cred_rotation/new_creds.json
           that satisfies **exactly the same schema** but with the following differences:

              • credentials.version      must be 2                (integer)
              • credentials.created_at   must be the current UTC  timestamp in extended
                                          ISO-8601 format          “YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ”
              • credentials.access_key   must match regex          ^AKIA[0-9A-Z]{8,}$
              • credentials.secret_key   must be 32 lowercase hexadecimal characters
                                          (regex ^[0-9a-f]{32}$)

        c. Append an audit line to the rotation log (see next point).

    3.  Required log file
        Every important step has to be recorded (append-only) in
            /home/user/cred_rotation/rotation.log
        Each line must follow *exactly* this structure:

            YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ <ACTION>: <DETAILS>

        where
            <ACTION>   is either “VALIDATION” or “ROTATION”
            <DETAILS>  is free text without newline characters.

        For this task the file must end up containing **exactly two lines**:

            1. A line documenting that old_creds.json passed validation, using the literal
               phrase “old_creds.json passed schema check”.
            2. A line documenting that new_creds.json was created, using the literal phrase
               “new_creds.json generated version 2”.

        Example (timestamps will obviously differ):

            2024-03-12T17:22:01Z VALIDATION: old_creds.json passed schema check
            2024-03-12T17:22:02Z ROTATION: new_creds.json generated version 2

    4.  Required verification artefacts
        After you are done the system **must** contain:

        • /home/user/cred_rotation/new_creds.json          (valid against the schema)
        • /home/user/cred_rotation/rotation.log            (exactly two correctly formatted lines)

    5.  Output
        Besides creating/updating the files above, print nothing except whatever normal console
        output your commands naturally produce.  The automated test harness will only inspect the
        two artefacts listed in section 4.

    Hints (not enforced):
        • jq can be used to do simple structural validation and regex checks.
        • date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ gives a compliant UTC timestamp.
        • Use atomic file creation (write to temp file then mv) to avoid partial writes.

    Success criteria that the grader will check:
        1. new_creds.json exists and jq '.credentials.version' returns 2.
        2. jq -e 'test("AKIA[0-9A-Z]{8,}"; "x")' over .credentials.access_key returns 0.
        3. jq -e 'test("^[0-9a-f]{32}$"; "x")' over .credentials.secret_key returns 0.
        4. .credentials.created_at matches extended ISO-8601 UTC format and ends with “Z”.
        5. rotation.log exists, is two lines long, and each line matches the exact format given
           in section 3 with the required literal phrases.
